fuzzy_prd:
We need a dependency injection container that allows teams to register value factories and automatically resolve the full dependency graph at runtime. Developers declare what a factory produces and what it needs as inputs; the container wires everything together, building each value at most once and caching it for subsequent requests — similar to how our configuration subsystem lazily initialises shared clients.

The system must support several registration and resolution variants. A single factory can produce multiple distinct value types at once. Values of the same type can coexist when each is tagged with a unique label; label matching must be exact, so casing differences count as different labels. A consumer may declare a dependency as optional, in which case the container supplies an absent indicator rather than failing.

The system must detect circular wiring at registration time and reject the offending registration immediately, leaving earlier valid registrations intact. Misuse scenarios — registering a null factory, a non-callable value, invoking with a non-callable target, requesting a type with no registered factory, registering a duplicate factory for an already-known type, or a factory that reports failure at construction time — must each produce a distinct, well-defined error category. The error taxonomy should follow the same conventions used in our validation middleware layer. Each constructed instance carries an observable integer payload so tests can confirm exactly which instance was received.

trigger_keywords: what payload does a provider emit when no value is specified
default payload for provider without explicit value
how is the instance integer determined when value is omitted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a produced key has no explicit 'value' field and the provider step has no default 'value' field, the payload is the number of times that provider has constructed so far (i.e., it increments on each construction call: 1 on the first call, 2 on the second, etc.). Because each value is cached after first construction, repeated invocations all see payload 1.

trigger_keywords: payload priority order for produced instance
which value wins when both per-key and provider-level value are set
precedence of value fields in provide step
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The payload for a produced instance is resolved in this priority order: (1) the 'value' field on the individual output key spec takes precedence; (2) if that is absent, the provider-level default 'value' field is used; (3) if neither is given, the payload is the running construction count for that provider.

trigger_keywords: success output format for provide step
what does a successful provide print
format of keys in provide ok line
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: On success, a provide step emits exactly: 'provide ok keys=<comma-separated keys>\n'. Each key is the bare type string (e.g., 'A') for unnamed outputs, or 'type:name' (e.g., 'A:first') for named outputs. Keys appear in the order they were declared in the 'out' array. Multiple keys are joined with commas and no spaces.

trigger_keywords: success output format for invoke step
what does a successful invoke print
format of key=value pairs in invoke ok line
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: On success, an invoke step emits exactly: 'invoke ok <space-separated key=payload pairs>\n'. Each pair is 'key=<integer>' for a present value, or 'key=nil' for an absent optional. Pairs appear in the order the requests were declared in the 'in' array, separated by single spaces.

trigger_keywords: what error strings can provide emit
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The provide step emits one of these exact error strings: 'provide error=nil_constructor' (null/untyped-nil constructor), 'provide error=not_a_constructor' (non-callable value), 'provide error=cycle' (registration would close a dependency cycle), 'provide error=duplicate_type' (a key is already registered in the container).

trigger_keywords: what error strings can invoke emit
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The invoke step emits one of these exact error strings: 'invoke error=nil_function' (null/untyped-nil target), 'invoke error=not_a_function' (non-callable target), 'invoke error=missing_dependency key=<key>' (required type not in container, includes the first missing key), 'invoke error=constructor_failed' (a constructor returned an error during construction).

trigger_keywords: does cycle error happen at provide or invoke time
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Cycles are detected at provide time, not at invoke time. When a new provider registration would close a cycle in the dependency graph, the provide step is rejected with 'provide error=cycle'. All earlier registrations that did not introduce a cycle remain valid and in effect.

trigger_keywords: is constructor failure detected at provide or invoke time
when does a failing constructor cause an error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A provider with 'fails: true' (simulating a constructor that returns an error) registers successfully — the provide step outputs 'provide ok keys=...' as normal. The error surfaces only when a consumer actually needs the value (lazily), resulting in 'invoke error=constructor_failed'. Registration never fails due to a constructor that would fail at runtime.

trigger_keywords: what happens if name case differs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Name matching is exact and case-sensitive. A provider registered with name 'CamelCase' cannot be resolved by requesting name 'camelcase' or any other case variant. Such a request fails with 'invoke error=missing_dependency key=<type>:<requested-name>'.

trigger_keywords: what key is reported in missing_dependency error
how is the missing key selected when multiple are absent
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When multiple required dependencies are missing, the error reports the first missing required key in request order (skipping optional requests). If all non-optional keys are technically present but one fails a secondary check, the fallback selects the first key from a sorted set of unprovided required keys. The key format is bare type (e.g., 'B') for unnamed, or 'type:name' (e.g., 'A:camelcase') for named requests.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how our configuration subsystem lazily initialises shared clients
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The singleton caching mechanism in dig.go where each value is constructed at most once and stored in the Container's cache map (map[key]reflect.Value), returned from cache on subsequent requests — analogous to lazy singleton initialisation patterns.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same conventions used in our validation middleware layer
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The error taxonomy defined in the dispatcher's classifyProvide and classifyInvoke functions in rcb_tests/dispatcher/main.go, which map native Go error messages to neutral category strings: nil_constructor, not_a_constructor, cycle, duplicate_type, nil_function, not_a_function, missing_dependency, constructor_failed.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That question does not match a known constraint in this oracle. Please consult the start.md specification or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for detailed behavioral contracts.

feature1_provide_and_invoke.json.description: Register one or more providers with the container, then run a consumer that requests values by their declared types. A provider declares the type(s) it produces and the type(s) it depends on; the container constructs each requested value on demand, resolving a provider's dependencies first (transitively). Each produced instance carries an integer payload, and the consumer reports, for each requested type in request order, the payload of the instance it received. This shows direct resolution and multi-level dependency chains where a requested value's provider depends on values produced by other providers.

cases.expected_output:
provide ok keys=A
invoke ok A=5

provide ok keys=A
provide ok keys=B
provide ok keys=C
invoke ok A=1 B=2 C=3

feature2_multiple_outputs.json.description: A single provider may declare several distinct produced types at once; registering it makes every one of those types resolvable from the container. The provide step lists all produced keys, and a later consumer can request any subset of them in any order, receiving each instance's payload. This shows that one registration can populate the graph with multiple independent types.

cases.expected_output:
provide ok keys=A,B
invoke ok A=10 B=20

feature3_singleton_cache.json.description: Resolved values are cached and effectively behave as singletons: a provider runs at most once, and every later request for the same type returns the already-constructed instance. To make the construction count observable, this provider omits an explicit payload; in that case the produced instance carries the number of times the provider has constructed so far (1 on the first construction, 2 on the second, and so on). Because the value is cached after the first construction, repeated consumers all observe the payload 1 — a provider that re-ran on every request would instead yield increasing payloads.

cases.expected_output:
provide ok keys=A
invoke ok A=1
invoke ok A=1

feature4_optional_dependency.json.description: A dependency request may be marked optional. When an optional type has no provider in the container, resolution does not fail; the consumer receives an absent value (reported as nil) and still runs. When the optional type IS provided, the consumer receives its payload as normal. This lets a consumer tolerate missing collaborators instead of failing outright.

cases.expected_output:
provide ok keys=A
invoke ok A=7

feature5_named_values.json.description: Several instances of the same type can coexist in the container when each is given a distinct name; a request then selects an instance by both its type and name. A named key (type:name) and an unnamed key of the same type are independent and may both be present at once. Name matching is exact and case-sensitive, so requesting a name that differs only in letter case finds no matching instance and resolution fails with a missing-dependency error naming the requested type:name.

cases.expected_output:
provide ok keys=A:first,A:second,A:third
invoke ok A:first=1 A:third=3

provide ok keys=A:CamelCase
invoke error=missing_dependency key=A:camelcase

feature6_cycle_detection.json.description: The dependency graph must remain acyclic. Registration of a provider whose dependencies would close a cycle (directly or through a chain of other providers) is rejected, and the offending provide step reports a cycle error. Earlier providers that did not yet complete a loop register normally; only the provide that introduces the cycle fails.

cases.expected_output:
provide ok keys=A
provide ok keys=B
provide error=cycle

feature7_1_missing_dependency.json.description: When a consumer requests a required (non-optional) type for which no provider exists, resolution fails with a missing-dependency error that names the unsatisfied key. The key is the bare type when unnamed, or type:name when a name was requested. If several required dependencies are missing, the error names one of them.

feature7_2_duplicate_type.json.description: Each type key may be produced by only one provider. Registering a second provider for a type that is already in the container is rejected; the duplicate provide step reports a duplicate-type error while the first registration remains in effect.

feature7_3_constructor_error.json.description: A provider may fail at construction time by reporting an error. Registration itself still succeeds, because the provider is only run when its value is actually needed. When a consumer (directly or transitively) requires a value whose provider fails during construction, resolution is aborted and the consumer reports a constructor-failed error; the consumer body is not run.

feature7_4_invalid_provide_target.json.description: A provider must be supplied as a constructor (a callable that produces values). Registering an absent/null provider, or a plain value that is not callable, is rejected up front. A null provider reports a nil-constructor error; any non-callable value reports a not-a-constructor error.

feature7_5_invalid_invoke_target.json.description: The consumer passed to an invocation must itself be a callable. Invoking with an absent/null target reports a nil-function error, and invoking with a value that is not callable reports a not-a-function error. Neither attempts any dependency resolution.
